title: Learning Diary
text:
Welcome to my Remote Sensing learning diary.
This site documents my conceptual and methodological development throughout the CASA0023 module. Each chapter represents one week of learning, moving from foundational principles of satellite observation to more advanced analytical and policy-oriented applications.
The diary emphasises critical understanding rather than code execution. While practical workflows are developed separately, this space focuses on how Earth Observation data can be interpreted, evaluated, and applied to urban and environmental decision-making.

title: Week 1 — Getting started
text:
Summary
This week introduced the basic ideas of remote sensing and how satellite data can be used to observe the Earth. The focus was on understanding what satellite images represent and how they differ from normal photographs. Instead of showing objects directly, remote sensing data records reflected energy from the Earth’s surface across different wavelengths. This helped me realise that satellite images are measurements rather than pictures.
Sentinel-2 data was used as the main example throughout the week. I learned that a single Sentinel-2 product contains multiple spectral bands, and each band responds differently to land cover features such as water, vegetation, and built-up areas. True colour images combine red, green, and blue bands to approximate human vision, while individual bands highlight specific surface properties.
True colour image used for initial visual interpretation of the study area.
Overall, this week built a foundation for understanding how satellite data is structured and why different visualisations are needed for different analytical purposes. It also clarified the difference between raw satellite data and processed visual outputs.

title: Week 1 — Getting started
text:
Applications
The practical work showed how remote sensing can support real-world analysis by revealing patterns that are not visible from ground-level observation. Using a single spectral band demonstrated how reflectance values vary across surfaces. For example, water areas appear very dark in the red band because water absorbs most of the incoming radiation, while snow and bare rock appear bright due to high reflectance.

Red band reflectance highlighting contrast between water, vegetation, and snow-covered terrain.
Combining multiple bands into an RGB composite improved visual clarity. Vegetated areas became easier to identify, and the spatial structure of coastlines and islands was clearer. This type of visualisation is useful for mapping, landscape interpretation, and comparison across regions.

RGB composite enhancing land cover contrast.
These methods are relevant for environmental monitoring, urban studies, and land-cover mapping. They show how satellite data can support decision-making by providing consistent spatial information over large areas.

title: Week 1 — Getting started
text:
Reflection
This week changed how I think about images in general. I used to see satellite images as similar to aerial photos, but I now understand that they are data products shaped by sensor design and processing choices. The same place can look very different depending on which bands are used and how they are combined.
I also realised that visual interpretation alone has limits. While RGB images are intuitive, they do not directly measure environmental conditions. This highlighted the importance of indices and quantitative approaches, which will become more important later in the course.
The practical tasks helped connect theory to actual data, but they also showed that careful interpretation is needed. Colours can be misleading if their meaning is not understood. This week made me more cautious and more critical when reading satellite imagery.

title: Week 1 — Getting started
section: Extended Reflection: From Visualisation to Analysis
text:
Extended Reflection: From Visualisation to Analysis
A key takeaway from this week was the difference between visual appearance and analytical value. Visual products are helpful for orientation and communication, but they are only the first step. For example, vegetation may look green in an RGB image, but this does not directly describe its condition or density.

NDVI map showing vegetation distribution across the study area.
The introduction of NDVI showed how combining bands mathematically can produce more meaningful information. Unlike RGB images, NDVI provides a clearer link to physical surface properties. This helped me understand why remote sensing is widely used in environmental and urban analysis.
This week laid the groundwork for later practical work. It showed that learning remote sensing is not only about tools, but also about thinking carefully about what the data represents and what questions it can realistically answer.

title: Week 2 — Portfolio
text: Week 2 Practical Slides

title: Week 2 — Portfolio
text:
Week 2 Learning Diary: Portfolio Tools

Summary of weekly task
This week focused on learning how to create and manage an online learning portfolio using Quarto and xaringan. The main task was to build a structured Quarto website and produce a short presentation in Markdown format, embedding the slides directly into the weekly notebook.


What I learned
Through working with xaringan, I learned how presentation slides can be created using R Markdown rather than traditional slide software. This approach integrates text, figures and explanations into a single, reproducible workflow, making it easier to update and maintain presentation content over time.
Using Quarto to organise weekly notebooks helped me understand the importance of project structure, file organisation and relative paths when building a multi-week portfolio. Embedding the slides into the Week 2 page highlighted how different outputs can be combined into a coherent and public-facing learning record.


Reflection
This week shifted my perspective from producing isolated files to thinking in terms of a reusable and extensible workflow. Although working with Quarto and xaringan initially required more effort than familiar tools, the approach offers clear advantages for documenting learning processes and maintaining transparency. I expect this structure to become increasingly valuable in later weeks, when more complex practical work needs to be clearly communicated and archived.

title: Week 3 — Corrections
text:
Summary
This week focused on the idea of corrections in remote sensing and why they are necessary before any meaningful analysis. Satellite images do not directly show surface conditions. Instead, they record energy that has travelled through the atmosphere, interacted with the surface, and been captured by a sensor. Because of this process, raw images include effects that are not related to the surface itself.
The lectures introduced three main types of correction: radiometric, atmospheric, and geometric correction. Radiometric correction explains how raw pixel values, stored as digital numbers, do not represent real physical properties. These values are influenced by the sensor and acquisition conditions. Converting them into radiance or reflectance makes images more comparable across space and time.
Atmospheric correction addresses the impact of scattering and absorption in the atmosphere. This is important because the atmosphere can make images appear brighter or more grey than the real surface. A simple method, Dark Object Subtraction, was introduced to estimate and remove this atmospheric effect using very dark features such as deep water or shadows.
Geometric correction focuses on spatial accuracy. Because of sensor angle, Earth curvature, and terrain effects, raw images may not align correctly with real-world locations. Correcting this allows satellite images to be used together with other spatial data, which is especially important in urban studies.

title: Week 3 — Corrections
text:
Applications
Corrections are widely used in studies that rely on Earth observation data for urban analysis. Many land cover and land use studies apply radiometric and atmospheric correction before classification to ensure that differences in pixel values represent real surface differences rather than sensor or atmospheric effects. This is particularly important when comparing images from different dates to study urban growth or land cover change.
In urban environments, atmospheric correction is often necessary because cities tend to have higher levels of air pollution and aerosols. These conditions increase scattering and can strongly affect image quality. Studies that use vegetation indices or surface reflectance products usually apply atmospheric correction to reduce these influences and improve the reliability of their results.
Geometric correction is also essential in practical applications. Urban remote sensing studies often combine satellite images with GIS layers such as road networks, building footprints, or administrative boundaries. Without geometric correction, these datasets may not align correctly, which can lead to errors in spatial analysis. For this reason, corrections are a standard part of operational workflows in urban remote sensing and planning-related studies.

title: Week 3 — Corrections
text:
Reflection
This week helped me realise how important data preparation is in remote sensing. Before learning about corrections, I mainly focused on what could be done with satellite images after they were loaded into software. The lectures showed that analysis results depend strongly on how the data are corrected at the beginning.
I also found that corrections are especially important for urban studies. Cities contain many different surface materials, strong shadows, and complex atmospheric conditions. These factors make urban imagery sensitive to error. Even simple correction methods can improve the reliability of analysis when more advanced data or models are not available.
Overall, this week made me more cautious when working with Earth observation data. It highlighted that understanding data limitations is as important as applying methods. I expect this awareness to be useful in later weeks of the course and in future work that combines remote sensing data with GIS or policy-related analysis.

title: Week 4 — Policy
text:
Introduction
Climate change presents long-term challenges for coastal cities, particularly through sea-level rise and storm surge intensification. Reykjavík, as a coastal capital city, contains economically significant waterfront areas, port infrastructure, transport links, and residential districts located in low-lying zones. While much of the coastline is already developed and partially protected, future climate uncertainty raises questions about the adequacy and prioritisation of structural coastal protection measures.
This study explores how scenario-based Earth Observation (EO) analysis can support protection-oriented climate adaptation planning in Reykjavík by identifying spatial exposure under plausible future sea-level rise conditions.

title: Week 4 — Policy
text:
Policy Context
At the municipal level, Reykjavík has adopted climate strategies aimed at strengthening resilience and adapting to long-term climate risks. Effective adaptation planning requires spatially explicit evidence identifying where exposure is most likely to increase under future scenarios.
Protection-focused adaptation (e.g., reinforcement of coastal infrastructure, seawalls, port defences) requires prioritisation. Limited public resources must be directed toward coastal segments where the concentration of vulnerable urban assets is highest. EO-derived scenario modelling provides a transparent and reproducible basis for such prioritisation.

title: Week 4 — Policy
text:
Scenario-Based Future Inundation Modelling
Rather than producing deterministic forecasts, this analysis applies a stress-testing framework using two sea-level rise scenarios:

Scenario 1: +0.5 m sea-level rise

Scenario 2: +1.0 m sea-level rise

These scenarios function as policy benchmarks, allowing planners to evaluate how exposure patterns shift as climate pressure increases.

title: Week 4 — Policy
text:
Data Framework
The analysis integrates three core spatial components:

1. Elevation Data (DEM)
A Digital Elevation Model (e.g., Copernicus DEM or ArcticDEM) is used to identify low-lying coastal areas potentially inundated under each sea-level scenario.


2. Coastal Delineation
Optical satellite imagery (Sentinel-2 or Landsat) supports shoreline validation and accurate separation between terrestrial and marine surfaces.


3. Exposure Layer (Built-Up Areas)
Built-up land is extracted from optical imagery (e.g., using NDBI or land cover classifications) to approximate the spatial distribution of urban assets potentially at risk.

title: Week 4 — Policy
text:
Method: Screening Inundation Model
A threshold-based “bathtub” inundation model is implemented:

Areas where elevation ≤ scenario sea-level increment are classified as potentially inundated.
A coastal connectivity constraint ensures that only low-lying areas connected to the sea are retained.

While this simplified approach does not simulate hydrodynamic processes such as wave action or storm surge dynamics, it provides a transparent and replicable first-stage risk screening suitable for strategic policy planning.

title: Week 4 — Policy
text:
Policy-Relevant Outputs
The scenario modelling generates planning indicators including:

Total potential inundation area (km²) under each scenario

Exposed built-up area (km² and %)

Identification of high-exposure coastal segments

These outputs support protection-oriented adaptation by identifying which stretches of coastline contain the highest concentration of vulnerable assets, thereby informing prioritisation of structural coastal reinforcement.

title: Week 4 — Policy
text:
Policy Implications: Protection-Oriented Adaptation
The results do not prescribe specific engineering interventions but provide an evidence base to guide decision-making. Scenario-based exposure mapping enables:

Prioritisation of structural protection investments

Integration of coastal risk zones into spatial planning

Strengthening of infrastructure resilience strategies

By translating uncertain climate futures into spatially explicit exposure maps, EO becomes a decision-support tool within urban climate governance.

title: Week 4 — Policy
text:
Alignment with Global Frameworks
This analysis aligns with international sustainable development objectives:

SDG 11.5: By identifying areas where coastal hazards may affect urban assets, the study contributes to reducing the potential impacts of climate-related disasters on cities.
SDG 11.b: The integration of spatial risk evidence into planning processes supports integrated disaster risk management.
SDG 13.1: Scenario-based modelling strengthens climate adaptation capacity and enhances resilience to climate-related hazards.

Through this local-to-global linkage, Earth Observation supports multi-level governance by connecting municipal adaptation planning to broader climate resilience frameworks.

title: Week 4 — Policy
text:
Reflection
This exercise highlighted the importance of framing remote sensing within governance rather than purely technical analysis. Scenario-based modelling is valuable not because it predicts the future with certainty, but because it structures uncertainty in a way that supports planning decisions.
Even a simplified inundation model can meaningfully inform policy if it is transparent, reproducible, and clearly aligned with planning objectives. At the same time, recognising methodological limitations is essential. The screening approach does not account for complex hydrodynamic processes, meaning outputs should be interpreted as indicative rather than definitive. This reinforces the need to position EO analysis as decision-support evidence within broader adaptation strategies rather than as a standalone solution.

title: Week 5 — GEE
text:
Summary
This week’s practical focused on multi-temporal image compositing and vegetation index analysis using Landsat 8 Collection 2 Level 2 surface reflectance data in Google Earth Engine (GEE). I selected Ho Chi Minh City (2023–2024) as my study area in order to explore how different compositing strategies influence image clarity and NDVI derivation in a humid tropical urban environment.
The workflow began by filtering the Landsat 8 image collection by spatial boundary (Ho Chi Minh City administrative boundary) and temporal range (January 2023 to December 2024). A scene-level cloud cover threshold (<40%) was applied to exclude highly contaminated scenes. Surface reflectance scale factors were then implemented to convert raw digital numbers into physically meaningful reflectance values.
To evaluate compositing strategies, I first generated a baseline RGB composite using a simple temporal median without applying pixel-level QA cloud masking. This image allowed me to observe how much atmospheric contamination remains when only scene-level filtering is used. While the urban structure was clearly visible, subtle haze and tonal inconsistencies were still present, especially in coastal and vegetated areas.
Next, I applied QA bitmask filtering to remove cloud-contaminated pixels prior to compositing. In addition, I calculated NDVI and implemented an NDVI-based qualityMosaic approach, which prioritises pixels with higher vegetation values when constructing the final composite. This produced a noticeably clearer RGB image with improved contrast and spatial consistency.
Finally, NDVI was derived from the refined composite using the standard formulation (NIR − Red) / (NIR + Red). The resulting NDVI map clearly distinguishes vegetated areas, dense urban cores, and water bodies across the metropolitan region.
This comparison demonstrates that compositing strategy selection significantly influences both visual quality and analytical outputs in tropical remote sensing applications.

title: Week 5 — GEE
text:
Figure 1. Administrative boundary of Ho Chi Minh City used as the spatial extent of analysis.






Figure 2. Median RGB composite without pixel-level cloud masking. Residual haze and tonal inconsistencies remain visible.






Figure 3. NDVI-based quality mosaic composite showing improved clarity and reduced cloud artefacts.






Figure 4. NDVI map derived from the quality mosaic composite. Higher values represent vegetation while lower values correspond to built-up areas and water.

title: Week 5 — GEE
text:
Applications
Cloud contamination remains one of the primary constraints in optical remote sensing, particularly in equatorial and tropical regions where persistent cloud cover limits usable observations. Many studies relying on Landsat or Sentinel imagery implement pixel-level quality masking and multi-temporal compositing to overcome this limitation. Roy et al. (2014) emphasise the importance of the Landsat Collection quality assessment bands for maintaining radiometric consistency and reliability across time series datasets. Automated cloud detection approaches, such as those discussed by Zhu and Woodcock (2012), further enhance the robustness of compositing workflows.
In urban environmental research, improved compositing directly affects downstream analyses such as land cover classification, urban heat island mapping, vegetation monitoring, and floodplain delineation. NDVI-based compositing is particularly useful in ecological and urban greening studies because it prioritises vegetation-rich pixels, often resulting in visually cleaner outputs. However, this method may introduce subtle bias by favouring vegetated surfaces over built-up areas, potentially influencing spectral representation in dense urban cores. This highlights the importance of methodological transparency when integrating Earth Observation data into environmental monitoring frameworks or policy-driven assessments.

title: Week 5 — GEE
text:
Reflection
This practical changed my perception of image compositing from being a routine preprocessing step to a methodological decision that shapes analytical interpretation. In a cloud-prone tropical city such as Ho Chi Minh City, simple median compositing is insufficient for producing stable outputs. While NDVI-based quality mosaicking clearly improves image clarity, it may also prioritise certain land cover types, influencing downstream analyses. In future work, I would consider comparing percentile-based or median-of-best-pixels approaches to further evaluate compositing trade-offs. Overall, this exercise strengthened my understanding of how preprocessing decisions directly affect the reliability and credibility of urban remote sensing analysis.

title: Week 6 — Classification1
text:
Summary
This week’s practical focused on supervised image classification using Sentinel-2 imagery in Google Earth Engine (GEE). The aim of the exercise was to understand how satellite imagery can be processed and classified into different land-cover types using machine learning methods.
I selected Guangzhou, China as the study area because it is my hometown. Using a familiar city made it easier to recognise important geographical features such as the Pearl River, urban districts and mountainous areas. Guangzhou is located in the Pearl River Delta and contains a mixture of dense urban areas, vegetation, rivers and construction sites, making it suitable for land-cover classification.



Study area boundary and training polygons


To generate cloud-free Sentinel-2 imagery, two different compositing approaches were tested.
The first approach filtered scenes using the CLOUDY_PIXEL_PERCENTAGE metadata variable and retained only images with less than 5% cloud cover before generating a median composite. This approach is computationally simple but may discard many potentially useful images.



Way One composite image


The second approach applied pixel-level cloud masking using the QA60 band to remove cloud and cirrus pixels before creating the composite image. Compared with scene-level filtering, this method allows more images to be used while masking only cloudy pixels, which generally results in a more complete composite.



Way Two composite image


After generating the composite image, training polygons were manually digitised for five land-cover classes: urban_high, urban_low, vegetation, water and bare soil. These training samples were used to extract spectral information from Sentinel-2 bands and train a CART (Classification and Regression Tree) classifier.
The trained model was then applied to classify the entire study area.



Classification result


The classification map shows several clear spatial patterns. Vegetation is mainly distributed in the mountainous northern areas of Guangzhou, while the central and southern parts of the city are dominated by urban land cover. Major water bodies such as the Pearl River are also clearly detected in the classification result.



Applications
Supervised classification is widely used in remote sensing to map land cover and monitor environmental change. With platforms such as Google Earth Engine, large satellite datasets can be processed efficiently without downloading imagery locally.
This type of workflow can be applied to analyse urban expansion, vegetation change or land-use dynamics over time. In rapidly developing cities such as Guangzhou, remote sensing classification could help identify patterns of urban growth and environmental change. Such analyses can support urban planning, environmental monitoring and sustainability studies.
In addition, cloud-based geospatial platforms significantly simplify the remote sensing workflow. Image filtering, cloud masking, training sample creation and classification can all be conducted within a single environment, which greatly improves analytical efficiency.



Reflection
This practical helped me better understand the workflow of supervised classification using satellite imagery. During my undergraduate studies I performed similar classification tasks using ArcGIS, where the general concept is comparable but the workflow is more fragmented. In ArcGIS, satellite imagery often needs to be downloaded and pre-processed locally before classification can be applied. In contrast, Google Earth Engine provides direct access to large satellite datasets and allows the entire process to be performed in the cloud, making the workflow much more efficient.
However, the classification results also reveal some limitations. In particular, the distinction between urban_high and urban_low is not very clear, and many areas appear to be classified as urban_high. This likely reflects the fact that the spectral characteristics of different urban surfaces are quite similar in Sentinel-2 imagery, which makes it difficult for a simple CART classifier to separate them accurately.
In future analyses, this limitation could potentially be improved by increasing the number of training samples, refining the training polygons, or applying more advanced classifiers such as Random Forest. Despite these challenges, the exercise demonstrates how supervised classification can quickly produce land-cover maps at the city scale.

title: Week 7 — Classification2
text: Week 7 – Classification II

title: Week 7 — Classification2
text:
Introduction
This week focused on more advanced classification techniques in Google Earth Engine, particularly spectral unmixing and sub-pixel classification. Compared to Week 6, where each pixel was assigned a single class, this method allows each pixel to contain multiple land cover types. This is especially useful in urban areas where land cover is often mixed.
For this week, I initially decided to use the Netherlands as my study area. One reason for choosing the Netherlands is that I am planning to travel there during the Easter holiday. Using a location that I am interested in made the learning process more engaging and meaningful.

title: Week 7 — Classification2
text:
Study Area Selection
At first, I attempted to classify the entire Netherlands.



Netherlands boundary


However, I quickly realised that using such a large study area created several challenges. The classification process became slow, and the results were less reliable. The Netherlands contains diverse landscapes including agricultural land, urban areas, wetlands and coastal environments. This made it difficult to create representative training samples.
To better understand the spatial variation, I visualised satellite imagery for the entire Netherlands.



Netherlands satellite


From this image, it became clear that using the entire country was not practical. Therefore, I decided to narrow the study area.

title: Week 7 — Classification2
text:
Focusing on Amsterdam
After reviewing the dataset, I selected Amsterdam and its surrounding areas as my final study area.



Amsterdam satellite


This region contains a mix of urban areas, vegetation, water bodies and agricultural land. This makes it suitable for classification exercises. I then manually digitised training polygons for four land cover classes:

Water

Urban

Vegetation

Agriculture

This step followed the supervised classification workflow introduced in Week 6.

title: Week 7 — Classification2
text:
Supervised Classification
After generating training samples, I applied Random Forest classification to produce a land cover map.



Classification result


The classification result shows that urban areas are concentrated in central Amsterdam, while agricultural land dominates surrounding regions. Water bodies were also clearly identified. However, I noticed that some mixed areas were poorly classified. This is because traditional classification forces each pixel to belong to only one category.
This limitation led to the introduction of spectral unmixing, which is the main focus of Week 7.

title: Week 7 — Classification2
text:
Spectral Unmixing
To improve classification accuracy, I applied spectral unmixing. This method estimates the proportion of different land cover types within each pixel.



Fraction map


This figure shows mixed land cover distributions. For example, urban fringe areas contain a mixture of vegetation and built-up land. These mixed pixels were not captured in the previous classification.
Compared to Week 6, this method provides more detailed information about land cover composition.

title: Week 7 — Classification2
text:
Fraction Map
To further explore the spectral unmixing results, I visualised a composite fraction map using RGB colours. In this map, red represents urban fraction, green represents vegetation fraction, and blue represents water fraction.



Fraction composite map


This composite map reveals mixed land cover patterns more clearly than traditional classification. Areas with strong red tones indicate dominant urban land cover, while green areas represent vegetation-dominated regions, and blue areas correspond to water bodies. Mixed colours, such as yellow or purple, indicate pixels containing multiple land cover types.
For example, urban fringe areas often appear as mixed colours, suggesting a combination of built-up land and vegetation. This demonstrates how spectral unmixing captures sub-pixel variability that was not visible in the Week 6 classification.
Compared to traditional classification, spectral unmixing provides more detailed spatial information and helps identify transitional areas between land cover types.

title: Week 7 — Classification2
text:
Reflection
This week was more challenging compared to previous weeks, but also more interesting. At the beginning, I tried to classify the entire Netherlands because I thought using a larger area would make the analysis more meaningful. However, I quickly realised that this made the processing slower and more complicated. This helped me understand the importance of selecting an appropriate study area.
Another challenge was understanding spectral unmixing. Initially, I was confused because the results looked very different from the classification map. The colour combinations were more complex, and I did not immediately understand that these maps represented proportions instead of categories. After comparing the fraction maps with the classification results, I gradually understood the concept of mixed pixels.
I also encountered several technical issues, such as undefined variables and memory limitations. These errors slowed down my workflow, but they helped me better understand how Google Earth Engine works. Solving these problems also improved my confidence in using GEE.
Choosing the Netherlands as my study area also made the learning process more engaging. Since I am planning to visit Amsterdam during Easter, analysing this region felt more meaningful. It was interesting to observe land cover patterns in a place that I might soon explore in person.
Overall, this week helped me understand the limitations of traditional classification and the advantages of sub-pixel analysis. This method appears particularly useful in urban environments where land cover is complex and mixed.

title: Week 7 — Classification2
text:
Additional Reading
To better understand spectral unmixing and sub-pixel classification, I reviewed several additional readings related to remote sensing classification.
Small (2004) explains spectral mixing in urban landscapes and shows how a single pixel may contain vegetation, impervious surfaces and soil. This helped me understand why some pixels in Amsterdam were difficult to classify using traditional methods.
Lu and Weng (2007) provide an overview of image classification approaches and discuss spectral mixture analysis. This reading helped me understand the advantages of sub-pixel classification in heterogeneous environments.
I also briefly reviewed Adams et al. (1995), which introduced the concept of spectral mixture analysis. This helped me better understand the theoretical background behind spectral unmixing.
Although I only reviewed key sections of these papers, they helped me better interpret my results and understand the advantages of Classification II.

title: Week 7 — Classification2
text:
References
Adams, J. B., Sabol, D. E., Kapos, V., Almeida Filho, R., Roberts, D. A., Smith, M. O., & Gillespie, A. R. (1995). Spectral mixture modeling: A new analysis of rock and soil types at the Viking Lander 1 site. Journal of Geophysical Research, 100(E4), 8097–8112.
Lu, D., & Weng, Q. (2007). A survey of image classification methods and techniques for improving classification performance. International Journal of Remote Sensing, 28(5), 823–870.
Small, C. (2004). The Landsat ETM+ spectral mixing space. Remote Sensing of Environment, 93(1–2), 1–17.
